questions.scenario: A request reaches `POST /v1/orders` without an application request ID. The service creates `req_01J8K2M6TK7S1E9R0Y6Q`, but downstream payment and database logs contain unrelated identifiers. What should the team change first?

options.text: Create a new request ID in each service and rely on close timestamps when asynchronous work overlaps.
Use a stable customer ID as the cross-service correlation key, exposing one identifier across unrelated requests.
Wait for complete distributed tracing before adding any correlation field to application logs.
Create a safe ID at the entry boundary, propagate the request context, and include that ID on important structured log events.

questions.explanation:
One safe identifier should follow the request so operators can connect application events even when a trace is absent. Independent IDs break the chain, customer data is unsafe as a correlation key, and useful log correlation does not depend on a complete tracing rollout.

Learn more in [AWS guidance for OpenTelemetry instrumentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-instrumenting-your-app.html).

questions.scenario: The orders API publishes an SQS message after checkout. The producer trace ends at `sqs.send`, and the Lambda consumer starts a new unrelated trace even though the business event is the same. Which change best restores the request path?

options.text: Increase the global trace sampling rate so unrelated traces are more likely to appear together.
Use the Lambda invocation request ID as the parent ID in the producer before the message is sent.
Inject trace context into message metadata and extract it before the consumer creates its processing span.
Copy the complete checkout request body into both services' logs and correlate on matching payload text.

questions.explanation:
An asynchronous boundary needs an explicit context handoff. Injecting and extracting trace context lets the consumer continue the causal path. More sampling does not create parentage, a future Lambda ID cannot parent the producer, and copying request bodies creates privacy risk without valid trace relationships.

Learn more in [Tracing Amazon SQS requests with X-Ray](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-services-sqs.html).

questions.scenario:
One checkout trace shows this timing:

```text
POST /v1/orders              1143 ms
  payment.authorize            51 ms
  rds.insert_order           1030 ms
  dynamodb.put_idempotency      12 ms
  s3.write_receipt              31 ms
```

Fleet-level RDS latency is normal for the same five-minute window. What is the best conclusion?

options.text: The trace proves the entire RDS fleet was unhealthy, so the database should be failed over immediately.
Treat normal fleet metrics as proof that the slow trace is instrumentation error.
The trace identifies a slow database span for this request; normal metrics make an isolated or query-specific cause worth checking next.
Instrument each helper before checking the correlated query and database log for this request.

questions.explanation:
Traces and metrics answer different scope questions. The trace localizes time for one request, while normal fleet metrics warn against declaring a service-wide database failure. The next evidence should be the query and logs correlated to this trace, not an immediate failover or more instrumentation.

Learn more in [AWS X-Ray concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-concepts.html).

questions.scenario: A service still uses the X-Ray SDK and X-Ray daemon. The team is planning the migration described in the article. Which two actions belong in the target design?

options.text: Instrument the application with OpenTelemetry and send OTLP data through the CloudWatch agent or an OpenTelemetry collector.
Remove all sampling during migration so trace counts become the release acceptance test.
Keep the X-Ray SDK as the permanent source and export only selected spans through OpenTelemetry, maintaining two instrumentation models.
Validate propagation and trace-to-log correlation before retiring the old instrumentation path.

questions.explanation:
AWS directs new and migrated tracing work toward OpenTelemetry, with an AWS-supported agent or collector exporting the data. Propagation and log correlation need verification before cutover. Keeping the legacy SDK as the permanent target defeats the migration, and disabling sampling confuses telemetry volume with correctness.

Learn more in [Migrating from X-Ray SDKs to OpenTelemetry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-sdk-migration.html).

questions.scenario: The service handles 30,000 routine health requests per minute and 40 payment requests per minute. Recording every trace is too expensive, but payment failures need strong evidence. Which sampling plan best matches the article?

options.text: Keep higher coverage for the important payment path and lower coverage for high-volume routine traffic, then review the rules as traffic changes.
Use one fixed sampling rate for routine health traffic and payment operations despite their different volume and diagnostic value.
Disable tracing on payment because low-volume routes do not produce statistically useful data.
Record all traces and remove correlated application logs to offset telemetry storage cost.

questions.explanation:
Sampling should reflect business risk and traffic volume. Important, low-volume payment work deserves stronger coverage than routine health traffic. A uniform low rate can miss the path that matters, no payment traces removes incident evidence, and deleting logs sacrifices a complementary signal instead of controlling trace volume.

Learn more in [X-Ray sampling rules](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-console-sampling.html).

questions.scenario: A trace shows `rds.insert_order` taking 4.9 seconds. The application log group has the likely timeout, but none of its JSON events include a trace ID or span ID. What is the most durable improvement?

options.text: Correlate by nearest timestamp and host name because those fields uniquely identify a distributed request.
Add trace and span identifiers from the active telemetry context to structured application logs.
Copy full span payloads into application logs, duplicating telemetry and increasing exposure and cost.
Correlate by timestamp and task ID, accepting collisions when concurrent requests share the same runtime.

questions.explanation:
Trace and span identifiers create a direct join between the slow span and the exact application events. Timestamps and hosts can collide, full trace payloads add noise and cost, and duration cannot identify a request. Request IDs can remain alongside trace context for support and business workflows.

Learn more in [CloudWatch trace-to-log correlation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/Application-Signals-TraceLogCorrelation.html).

questions.scenario: A new trace contains spans named `handler`, `aws`, and `db`. The associated logs say only `started`, `slow`, and `done`. During an incident, responders cannot tell which business operation or dependency call each span represents. What should the team improve first?

options.text: Add spans around private helpers while keeping generic operation names and no business attributes.
Attach customer email and payment token values as span attributes so each trace is unmistakable.
Remove application logs after tracing is enabled so responders have one telemetry source.
Rename spans for operations such as `checkout.authorize_payment` and `rds.insert_order`, then emit safe structured fields that tell the same request story.

questions.explanation:
Instrumentation should express meaningful operations and safe context. More generic spans add volume without clarity, sensitive attributes create security risk, and traces do not replace logs. Clear operation names plus structured correlation make both tools easier to use.

Learn more in [AWS guidance for OpenTelemetry instrumentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/xray-instrumenting-your-app.html).
